examples.explanation: Initial: [1,2,1]. Option1: remove [1] at index0 (1 point): new [2,1]. Then remove [2] (1): [1]. Then [1] (1): total 3. Option2: remove [2] at index1 (1): [1,1]. Then remove [1,1] (4): total 5. Option3: remove [1] at index2 (1): [1,2]. Then remove [1] (1): [2]. Then [2] (1): total 3. Option4: remove [1,1]? Not consecutive. So max is 5.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(n!)
approaches.summary: Try all possible sequences of removing boxes by recursively exploring every possible removal order. This approach considers all permutations of removal operations without any optimization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Remove Boxes
ladders.desc: Complete O(n!) brute force solution exploring all possible removal sequences for the Remove Boxes problem.
ladders.explanation: The Remove Boxes problem asks: given boxes colored with integers, you can remove consecutive boxes of the same color k times to earn k*k points. After removal, adjacent boxes become consecutive. Find maximum points. The brute force approach explores every possible sequence of removal operations without optimization. Intuition: Since each removal changes the sequence structure, we need to consider all permutations of removal orders to find the optimal one. We recursively try removing every possible contiguous group of same-colored boxes at each step, calculating points and continuing with the modified sequence. This explores the complete decision tree where each node represents a state (current sequence) and edges represent removal choices. The algorithm: 1. Base case: if sequence empty, return 0. 2. For each possible contiguous group of same-colored boxes in current sequence: a. Calculate points = length*length. b. Remove that group from sequence. c. Recursively solve remaining sequence. d. Total = points + recursive result. 3. Return maximum total across all choices. Time: O(n!) worst-case as each removal creates new sequences to explore. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and sequence storage. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive exploration subroutine as its core engine. Common mistakes: Not considering all possible groups (must check every starting position and length), incorrect sequence modification after removal, missing base case. Real pattern: def solve(seq): if not seq: return 0; max_points = 0; for i in range(len(seq)): for j in range(i, len(seq)): if seq[i] == seq[j]: group = seq[i:j+1]; points = len(group)**2; new_seq = seq[:i] + seq[j+1:]; max_points = max(max_points, points + solve(new_seq)); return max_points.

examples.trace: Initial: [1,2,1]. Option1: remove [1] at index0 (1 point): new [2,1]. Then remove [2] (1): [1]. Then [1] (1): total 3. Option2: remove [2] at index1 (1): [1,1]. Then remove [1,1] (4): total 5. Option3: remove [1] at index2 (1): [1,2]. Then remove [1] (1): [2]. Then [2] (1): total 3. Option4: remove [1,1]? Not consecutive. So max is 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Exploration of Removal Sequences
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores all possible removal choices from a given sequence.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach. Given a sequence of boxes, it recursively tries removing every possible contiguous group of same-colored boxes, returning the maximum points achievable from that state. Intuition: At any point, the optimal solution from the current sequence is the maximum over all possible immediate removal choices, plus the optimal solution from the resulting sequence. This is a classic recursive decision tree exploration. The algorithm: function explore(seq): if seq is empty: return 0; max_points = 0; for start = 0 to len(seq)-1: for end = start to len(seq)-1: if all boxes from start to end have same color: points = (end-start+1)^2; new_seq = remove boxes from start to end from seq; total = points + explore(new_seq); max_points = max(max_points, total); return max_points. Time: O(n! * n^2) in worst case because for each sequence (up to n! leaves), we check O(n^2) groups. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and sequence copies. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called recursively in the full solution. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of trying all groups. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check all groups (must use nested loops), incorrect base case, not copying sequence properly when removing. Real pattern: def explore(seq): if not seq: return 0; best = 0; n = len(seq); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): if all(seq[k] == seq[i] for k in range(i, j+1)): group_len = j-i+1; points = group_len * group_len; new_seq = seq[:i] + seq[j+1:]; best = max(best, points + explore(new_seq)); return best.

examples.trace: Call explore([1,2,1]). Not empty. Check groups: start=0,end=0: color 1, points=1, new_seq=[2,1], recursive call explore([2,1]) returns max: from [2,1], options: remove [2] (1) then [1] (1) total 2, or remove [1] (1) then [2] (1) total 2, so returns 2. Total=1+2=3. start=0,end=1: colors 1 and 2 different, skip. start=0,end=2: colors not all same, skip. start=1,end=1: color 2, points=1, new_seq=[1,1], explore([1,1]) returns 4 (remove group [1,1]), total=1+4=5. start=1,end=2: colors 2 and 1 different, skip. start=2,end=2: color 1, points=1, new_seq=[1,2], explore([1,2]) returns 2 (remove individually), total=1+2=3. Max is 5.

Call explore([1,1]). Groups: start=0,end=0: points=1, new_seq=[1], explore([1]) returns 1, total=2. start=0,end=1: same color, points=4, new_seq=[], explore([]) returns 0, total=4. start=1,end=1: points=1, new_seq=[1], explore([1]) returns 1, total=2. Max is 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trying All Contiguous Same-Color Groups
ladders.desc: Identifying and evaluating every possible contiguous group of identical boxes in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: at each step, we must consider removing every contiguous subsequence where all boxes have the same color. Intuition: Since points are k*k for removing k boxes of same color, and removal can make previously non-consecutive boxes become adjacent, we need to evaluate all possible groups that could be removed now. The algorithm: Given sequence seq of length n, for each start index i from 0 to n-1, for each end index j from i to n-1, check if seq[i] == seq[i+1] == ... == seq[j]. If yes, this is a valid group of length len = j-i+1, worth len*len points. We must check all such (i,j) pairs. Time: O(n^3) if checking naively (n^2 pairs, each check O(n)), but can be optimized to O(n^2) by precomputing or breaking early. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is used inside the recursive explore function to generate removal options. Connection to L3: It relies on iterating through indices and comparing values. Common mistakes: Not checking all pairs (e.g., missing when i>j), incorrect equality check, off-by-one errors in indices. Real pattern: n = len(seq); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): all_same = True; for k in range(i, j+1): if seq[k] != seq[i]: all_same = False; break; if all_same: group_len = j-i+1; points = group_len * group_len; # use points.

examples.trace: Sequence length 4. i=0: j=0: [1] same, len=1, points=1. j=1: [1,2] not same. j=2: [1,2,2] not same. j=3: [1,2,2,1] not same. i=1: j=1: [2] same, len=1, points=1. j=2: [2,2] same, len=2, points=4. j=3: [2,2,1] not same. i=2: j=2: [2] same, len=1, points=1. j=3: [2,1] not same. i=3: j=3: [1] same, len=1, points=1. Total groups found: (0,0), (1,1), (1,2), (2,2), (3,3).

ladders.title: Building Block — Sequence Manipulation After Removal
ladders.desc: Creating a new sequence by removing a contiguous subarray from an array.
ladders.explanation: After choosing a group to remove, we need to generate the new sequence for recursive calls. This involves removing elements from index start to end inclusive from the original sequence. Intuition: Since sequences are represented as lists, we can create a new list by concatenating the prefix (elements before start) and the suffix (elements after end). This operation must preserve order and correctly handle indices. Algorithm: Given sequence seq, start index i, end index j (0-based, inclusive), new_seq = seq[0:i] + seq[j+1:len(seq)]. This uses list slicing. Time: O(n) to copy elements. Space: O(n) for new list. Connection to L2: This is used after identifying a group to remove. Connection to L4: It relies on basic list operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect slicing indices (off-by-one), modifying original sequence in-place (which affects other recursive branches), not handling empty prefix/suffix correctly. Real pattern: def remove_group(seq, i, j): return seq[:i] + seq[j+1:].

examples.trace: Original: [1,2,3,4,5]. Prefix seq[0:1] = [1]. Suffix seq[3:5] = [4,5] (since j+1=3, len=5). Concatenate: [1] + [4,5] = [1,4,5].
Prefix seq[0:0] = []. Suffix seq[3:3] = []. Concatenate: [] + [] = [].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calculating Points for a Group
ladders.desc: Computing k*k points for removing k boxes of the same color.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes the score earned when removing a group of k consecutive boxes with the same color. Intuition: The problem awards points as k*k to incentivize removing larger groups together rather than individually (since k*k >= k for k>=1, with strict inequality for k>1). For example, removing 3 boxes together gives 9 points vs removing individually gives 3 points. Algorithm: Given group length k (integer >=1), points = k * k. Simply square the length. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This points value is used when evaluating a removal group. Connection to L5: It uses basic arithmetic. Common mistakes: Using k*2 instead of k*k, incorrect for k=0 (but groups have at least 1 box). Real pattern: def calculate_points(k): return k * k.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Identifying Contiguous Same Values in an Array
ladders.desc: Finding all subarrays where all elements are equal.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: given an array, identify every contiguous subarray (sequence of consecutive elements) where all values are identical. Intuition: We need to check each possible starting index and extend while values remain the same. This is foundational for detecting removable groups in the boxes problem. Algorithm: Let arr be an array of length n. For each start index i from 0 to n-1: set current value = arr[i]; for each end index j from i to n-1: if arr[j] == current value, then subarray from i to j has all same values; else break. This yields all contiguous same-value subarrays. Time: O(n^2) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This operation is used to find groups before calculating points. Common mistakes: Not checking all start indices, not breaking when value changes, off-by-one in loop bounds. Real pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): val = arr[i]; for j in range(i, n): if arr[j] == val: # subarray i..j is valid; else: break.

examples.trace: Start i=0, val=1. j=0: arr[0]=1 same, subarray (0,0). j=1: arr[1]=1 same, subarray (0,1). j=2: arr[2]=2 different, break. i=1, val=1. j=1: same, (1,1). j=2: different, break. i=2, val=2. j=2: same, (2,2).

approaches.name: Basic Interval DP O(n^4)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming with state dp[l][r] representing the maximum points from subarray boxes[l..r]. This approach breaks the problem into smaller intervals but doesn't handle merging of same-colored boxes optimally.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Boxes with Basic Interval DP O(n⁴)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with state dp[l][r] for maximum points from boxes[l..r].
ladders.explanation: This problem involves removing boxes where you gain points equal to k² when removing k consecutive boxes of the same color. The basic interval DP approach defines dp[l][r] as the maximum points obtainable from the subarray boxes[l..r]. The intuition is to consider all possible splits and removals within the interval. For each interval [l,r], we try all possible partitions k where l ≤ k ≤ r. The recurrence is: dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r]) for all k, but this alone doesn't handle merging same-colored boxes optimally. To incorporate color merging, we need to consider when boxes[k] matches boxes[l], allowing potential merging with boxes outside [l,r]. However, this basic approach doesn't track additional states for merging, leading to suboptimal solutions in some cases. The algorithm initializes dp[i][i] = 1 for all i. Then, for length from 2 to n, for all l, compute r = l+len-1, and compute dp[l][r] using the recurrence. The time complexity is O(n⁴) because for each interval O(n²), we consider O(n) splits and for each split potentially O(n) operations for merging checks. Space is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This uses the interval splitting logic as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not initializing base cases correctly, missing the merging consideration, and incorrect loop bounds. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[n][n] with zeros, set dp[i][i]=1. For len from 2 to n: for l from 0 to n-len: r = l+len-1; for k from l to r: compute max points.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[9][9]. Base: dp[i][i]=1. Len=2: [1,3] -> max(dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]=2, consider merge? none) so dp[0][1]=2. Similarly others. Len=3: [1,3,2] -> try splits: k=0: dp[0][0]+dp[1][2], compute dp[1][2] from len=2 as max( dp[1][1]+dp[2][2]=2, merge? none) =2, so 1+2=3; k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]+dp[2][2]? no, split at k means dp[0][0] and dp[1][2] but careful: recurrence is dp[l][r]=max(dp[l][k-1]+dp[k][r]), so for k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][2]=1+2=3; k=2: dp[0][1]+dp[2][2]=2+1=3; so dp[0][2]=3. Continue for all lengths up to 9. Final dp[0][8] computed: for len=9, l=0,r=8, try k from 0 to 8. Key splits: when boxes match, e.g., boxes[2]=2, boxes[3]=2, boxes[4]=2, merging gives points. Compute: dp[0][8]=23 after considering all splits and potential merges (though basic DP may not capture optimal merge without extra state).

n=3. Initialize dp[i][i]=1. Len=2: [1,1] -> dp[0][1]: try k=0: dp[0][-1] invalid, so skip; k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]=2; but also consider merging since boxes[0]=boxes[1]=1, so can remove together for 2²=4 points? Basic DP might not handle this directly. Actually, for basic approach, we compute: dp[0][1]=max( for k=0: invalid, k=1: 1+1=2) so dp[0][1]=2. Len=3: [1,1,1] -> dp[0][2]: try k=0: invalid; k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][2]; compute dp[1][2] from len=2: dp[1][2]=2 as above; so 1+2=3; k=2: dp[0][1]+dp[2][2]=2+1=3; so dp[0][2]=3, but optimal is remove all three for 3²=9. This shows the basic DP fails without extra state for merging.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Interval Splitting for Maximum Points
ladders.desc: Compute maximum points for an interval by trying all possible split points.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the DP recurrence. Given an interval [l,r] in the boxes array, the goal is to find the maximum points by splitting it into two subintervals. The intuition is that any optimal removal sequence can be broken at some point k, where we first solve the left part [l,k-1] and then the right part [k,r], or vice versa. However, due to the merging of same-colored boxes, this split might not be independent, but in this basic approach, we assume independence for simplicity. The algorithm: For a given l and r, initialize maxPoints = 0. For each k from l to r, compute points = dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r], where dp is the memoized results for subintervals. Take the maximum over all k. Also, consider the case where boxes[k] matches boxes[l], allowing potential merging, but this basic version might handle it suboptimally. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) per interval, as we iterate over O(n) split points. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each interval in the main DP loop. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of breaking problems into subproblems. Common mistakes include incorrect indices for k (e.g., using k <= r instead of k <= r for splitting), and not handling base cases where l > r. Real code pattern: function computeInterval(l, r): if l > r: return 0; maxPts = 0; for k in range(l, r+1): pts = dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r]; maxPts = max(maxPts, pts); return maxPts.

examples.trace: Compute for interval [0,2]. Try k=0: dp[0][-1] invalid, assume 0 + dp[0][2]? no, recurrence is dp[l][k-1]+dp[k][r], so for k=0: dp[0][-1] (0) + dp[0][2] but dp[0][2] not known yet—circular. Actually, in DP, we compute bottom-up, so dp values for smaller intervals are known. Here, assume dp[0][2] is being computed. So for k=0: left = dp[0][-1]=0, right = dp[0][2] unknown, skip or use recursion? In iterative DP, we compute in order of length, so for len=3, dp[0][2] uses dp for len<=2. So: k=0: left=0, right=dp[0][2]? no, right should be dp[0][2] but that's same interval—invalid. Correct: k ranges from l to r, but split means left [l,k-1] and right [k,r]. For k=l, left is empty (0), right is [l,r]—same interval, so need to avoid or handle separately. Typically, we consider k such that split is valid, or include case where we remove box k separately. In basic DP, we might do: for k from l to r: if k == l: consider removing box l alone? But standard recurrence is dp[l][r] = max( dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r] ) for k from l to r, with dp[i][j] for i>j as 0. So for k=l: dp[l][l-1] (0) + dp[l][r]. But dp[l][r] is unknown, so this doesn't work directly. Actually, common approach: dp[l][r] = max( dp[l+1][r] + 1, ... ) for removing box l alone, and splits for k>l. So example: l=0,r=2, boxes=[1,2,3]. Compute: option1: remove box 0 alone: 1 + dp[1][2] = 1+2=3. option2: split at k=1: dp[0][0] + dp[1][2] = 1+2=3. option3: split at k=2: dp[0][1] + dp[2][2] = 2+1=3. So max=3.
Interval [0,1]. Option1: remove box 0 alone: 1 + dp[1][1] = 1+1=2. Option2: split at k=1: dp[0][0] + dp[1][1] = 1+1=2. So max=2. But optimal is remove together for 4 points, showing limitation.

testCases.input: l=0, r=4, boxes=[1,1,2,2,1], dp as per basic computation

ladders.title: Core Logic — Breaking Problem into Subintervals with DP
ladders.desc: The central technique of using dynamic programming on intervals to solve combinatorial problems.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves dividing the problem into overlapping subproblems based on intervals in the array. For the Remove Boxes problem, the state dp[l][r] represents the maximum points achievable from the subarray boxes[l..r]. The intuition is that the optimal solution for a larger interval can be constructed from optimal solutions of smaller intervals, by considering how to split the interval or how to remove elements. This is a classic interval DP pattern used in problems like matrix chain multiplication or palindrome partitioning. The key insight is that removal operations can be independent on disjoint subarrays, but merging of same-colored boxes introduces dependencies that require careful state design. In this basic approach, we assume independence for simplicity, leading to a recurrence like dp[l][r] = max( dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r] ) for all k in [l,r], plus base cases. The algorithm builds solutions bottom-up: first solve all intervals of length 1, then length 2, up to length n. Time complexity arises from O(n²) intervals and O(n) splits per interval, giving O(n³) for this core, but with merging checks it becomes O(n⁴). Space is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This logic defines how to compute each interval using the splitting subroutine. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding intervals and DP states. Common mistakes include not ordering computation by interval length, incorrect recurrence, and not handling empty intervals. Real code pattern: n = len(boxes); dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = 1; for length in range(2, n+1): for l in range(n-length+1): r = l+length-1; compute dp[l][r] using splits.

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp: dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1, dp[2][2]=1. Length=2: Interval [0,1]: l=0,r=1. Compute: option remove box0 alone: 1+dp[1][1]=2; split at k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]=2; so dp[0][1]=2. Interval [1,2]: l=1,r=2. Similarly: dp[1][2]=2. Length=3: Interval [0,2]: l=0,r=2. Compute: option remove box0 alone: 1+dp[1][2]=1+2=3; split at k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][2]=1+2=3; split at k=2: dp[0][1]+dp[2][2]=2+1=3; so dp[0][2]=3. Final answer dp[0][2]=3.

n=2. Initialize: dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1. Length=2: Interval [0,1]: l=0,r=1. Compute: remove box0 alone: 1+dp[1][1]=2; split at k=1: dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]=2; so dp[0][1]=2. But optimal is 4, showing issue.

ladders.title: Building Block — Interval Representation and DP State Definition
ladders.desc: Understanding how to represent subproblems as intervals and define DP states.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the concept of representing a subproblem by an interval [l,r] in an array. In dynamic programming, we often break down problems based on contiguous segments. For Remove Boxes, the state dp[l][r] is defined as the maximum points from removing boxes in the subarray from index l to r inclusive. The intuition is that the removal process for a contiguous segment can be solved independently if no boxes outside interfere, but due to merging, this independence is limited. The key is to identify that the optimal solution for [l,r] depends on solutions for smaller intervals within it. We define dp as a 2D array where indices l and r range from 0 to n-1. Base cases: dp[i][i] = 1 for a single box. For l > r, dp[l][r] = 0 (empty interval). The state definition allows us to use recurrence relations to compute larger intervals from smaller ones. Time and space for just defining states: O(n²) space for the table, no time yet. Connection to L2: This state definition enables the core DP logic. Connection to L4: It relies on array indexing and intervals. Common mistakes: using 1-indexing inconsistently, not handling empty intervals, or defining states that don't capture the problem structure. Real code pattern: n = len(boxes); dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = 1.

examples.trace: n=4. Define dp[0][0] for subarray [1], dp[1][1] for [2], dp[2][2] for [3], dp[3][3] for [4]. Also dp[0][1] for [1,2], dp[0][2] for [1,2,3], etc. All initialized to 0 except diagonals to 1.
n=2. dp[0][0]=1 for [1], dp[1][1]=1 for [1], dp[0][1] will be computed for [1,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Updating DP Table Entries
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading from and writing to a 2D DP table.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves manipulating a 2D array to store and retrieve dynamic programming states. For interval DP, we use a table dp where dp[l][r] holds the maximum points for the subarray from index l to r. The operations include: initializing the table, setting base values, reading values for smaller intervals to compute larger ones, and updating entries. The intuition is that DP relies on memoization to avoid recomputation, so efficient access and update are crucial. In Python, this is typically done using a list of lists. Steps: 1. Initialize a 2D list of size n x n with zeros. 2. Set diagonal entries dp[i][i] to 1 for single boxes. 3. In nested loops, for each interval [l,r], read dp[l][k-1] and dp[k][r] for various k to compute dp[l][r]. 4. Update dp[l][r] with the computed maximum. Time complexity per access/update is O(1). Space is O(n²) for the table. Connection to L3: These operations implement the state definition. Connection to L5: It builds on array indexing. Common mistakes: index out of bounds (e.g., accessing dp[l][r] with l>r), incorrect initialization, or using mutable lists improperly. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = 1; then in loops: val = dp[l][k-1] + dp[k][r]; dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], val).

examples.input: Update dp[0][2] using dp[0][1]=2 and dp[2][2]=1
examples.trace: Suppose computing for interval [0,2]. Read dp[0][1]=2 and dp[2][2]=1. Compute sum = 2+1=3. Set dp[0][2] = max(current dp[0][2], 3). If dp[0][2] was 0, it becomes 3.

testCases.input: Read dp[0][0] from initialized table for n=3
Read dp[1][2] from table where dp[1][1]=1, dp[2][2]=1, not computed yet
Update dp[0][3] using dp[0][1]=2 and dp[2][3]=2
Read dp[2][2] from initialized table for n=5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Intervals
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: understanding arrays, indices, and contiguous intervals.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers basic array concepts: an array is a collection of elements accessible by indices starting from 0. An interval [l,r] represents a contiguous subsequence from index l to r inclusive. For example, in array [A,B,C,D], interval [1,2] refers to [B,C]. The intuition is that many problems, including Remove Boxes, involve processing contiguous segments of an array. Key operations: accessing an element by index (e.g., boxes[i]), iterating over a range (e.g., for i in range(l, r+1)), and understanding length (r-l+1). This is essential for defining DP states as intervals. Time and space: accessing is O(1), iterating over an interval is O(length). Connection to L4: This enables DP table operations. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using r-l instead of r-l+1 for length), confusing 0-indexing with 1-indexing, or not handling empty intervals. Real code pattern: boxes = [1,2,3]; l=0; r=2; interval = boxes[l:r+1] gives [1,2,3]; length = r-l+1 = 3.

examples.trace: Indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40. Interval from l=1 to r=2 inclusive: elements at indices 1 and 2: 20 and 30. Length = r-l+1 = 2-1+1 = 2.
Only one element at index 0: 5. Interval [0,0] includes just this element. Length = 0-0+1 = 1.

approaches.name: Enhanced Interval DP with Extra Dimension O(n^4)
approaches.summary: Extend the DP state to dp[l][r][k] where k represents how many boxes of the same color as boxes[r] are attached after r. This allows optimal merging of same-colored boxes across intervals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Boxes with Enhanced Interval DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dp[l][r][k] to maximize points by removing boxes with same colors.
ladders.explanation: The Remove Boxes problem requires maximizing points by removing boxes where points equal (number of same-colored boxes removed)^2. The enhanced interval DP approach uses state dp[l][r][k], where l and r define the interval [l, r] being considered, and k represents how many boxes of the same color as boxes[r] are attached after r (i.e., boxes beyond r that match boxes[r]'s color). This extra dimension allows optimal merging of same-colored boxes across intervals, which is crucial because removing boxes of the same color together yields higher points due to the quadratic scoring. The intuition is to consider two cases: (1) Remove boxes[r] along with the k attached boxes of the same color immediately, or (2) Partition the interval at some index m where boxes[m] matches boxes[r], solve subproblems, and merge results. For case (1), we remove boxes[r] and the k attached boxes, earning (k+1)^2 points, plus the solution for the remaining interval [l, r-1] with 0 attached boxes. For case (2), we find all m in [l, r-1] where boxes[m] == boxes[r], and combine dp[l][m][k+1] (interval [l, m] with k+1 attached boxes of same color as boxes[r]) and dp[m+1][r-1][0] (interval [m+1, r-1] with 0 attached boxes). The maximum over these options gives dp[l][r][k]. The base case is when l > r, returning 0. We compute dp bottom-up for increasing interval lengths, with l from n-1 down to 0, r from l to n-1, and k from 0 to n-r-1. Time complexity is O(n^4) due to three nested loops for l, r, k and an inner loop over m. Space complexity is O(n^3) for the 3D DP table. This approach efficiently handles the non-local dependencies where same-colored boxes far apart can be removed together for optimal points. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of k (e.g., not considering attached boxes beyond r) or missing the partition case, leading to suboptimal solutions. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of computing dp for a given state by exploring all partitions. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: Initialize dp as 3D array of size n x n x n filled with 0. For l from n-1 down to 0: For r from l to n-1: For k from 0 to n-r-1: Option1 = (k+1)**2 + (dp[l][r-1][0] if r-1 >= l else 0). Option2 = max over m in [l, r-1] where boxes[m] == boxes[r] of dp[l][m][k+1] + dp[m+1][r-1][0]. dp[l][r][k] = max(Option1, Option2). Return dp[0][n-1][0].

examples.trace: Compute dp for n=9. Initialize dp[0..8][0..8][0..8]=0. Process intervals bottom-up. Key steps: For interval [0,8] with k=0: boxes[8]=1. Option1: remove boxes[8] with 0 attached -> 1^2=1 + dp[0][7][0]. dp[0][7][0] computed as 22 (from subproblems). Option1=23. Option2: find m where boxes[m]==1: m=0. Combine dp[0][0][1] and dp[1][7][0]. dp[0][0][1]=4 (since removing box 0 with 1 attached gives 2^2=4), dp[1][7][0]=19. Option2=23. Max=23. Other intervals contribute: e.g., [1,7] with k=0 yields 19 by removing 3's and 2's optimally. Final dp[0][8][0]=23.

n=3. dp[0][2][0]: boxes[2]=1. Option1: remove boxes[2] with 0 attached -> 1^2=1 + dp[0][1][0]. dp[0][1][0]: boxes[1]=1. Option1: 1 + dp[0][0][0]=1+1=2. Option2: m=0, combine dp[0][0][1] and dp[1][0][0]. dp[0][0][1]=4 (remove box 0 with 1 attached -> 2^2=4), dp[1][0][0]=0. Option2=4. So dp[0][1][0]=4. Thus Option1 for dp[0][2][0]=1+4=5. Option2: m=0, combine dp[0][0][2] and dp[1][1][0]. dp[0][0][2]=9 (remove box 0 with 2 attached -> 3^2=9), dp[1][1][0]=1. Option2=10. Option2: m=1, combine dp[0][1][2] and dp[2][1][0]. dp[0][1][2]=9 (remove boxes 0,1 with 2 attached -> 3^2=9), invalid interval. So max=10. But wait, recalc: Actually, dp[0][2][0]: Option1=5, Option2 for m=0: dp[0][0][1]=4, dp[1][1][0]=1 ->5, same. For m=1: dp[0][1][1]=? Compute dp[0][1][1]: boxes[1]=1. Option1: remove with 1 attached -> 2^2=4 + dp[0][0][0]=1 ->5. Option2: m=0, combine dp[0][0][2]=9 and dp[1][0][0]=0 ->9. So dp[0][1][1]=9. Then dp[2][1][0] invalid. So Option2 for m=1: 9+0=9. Max over options: 9. So dp[0][2][0]=9. Output 9.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing DP State for an Interval with Attached Boxes
ladders.desc: Compute dp[l][r][k] for a fixed interval [l, r] and k attached boxes of same color as boxes[r].
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the enhanced interval DP approach. Given an interval [l, r] and an integer k representing how many boxes of the same color as boxes[r] are attached after r, the goal is to compute the maximum points obtainable from this configuration. The intuition is to decide whether to remove boxes[r] immediately along with the k attached boxes, or to delay removal by partitioning the interval at a matching color to merge with the attached boxes later. This decision captures the optimal strategy for handling same-colored boxes that are not contiguous. The algorithm: First, handle base case: if l > r, return 0. Otherwise, consider two options. Option 1: Remove boxes[r] and the k attached boxes together. This yields (k+1)^2 points, plus the maximum points from the remaining interval [l, r-1] with 0 attached boxes (since boxes[r] is removed). Option 2: Partition the interval at some index m where l ≤ m ≤ r-1 and boxes[m] has the same color as boxes[r]. For each such m, we can combine two subproblems: (a) Solve for interval [l, m] with k+1 attached boxes (the original k plus boxes[r] treated as attached), and (b) Solve for interval [m+1, r-1] with 0 attached boxes (the segment between m and r, excluding boxes[r]). The points are the sum of these two subproblems. The maximum over all such m gives Option 2. Finally, dp[l][r][k] is the maximum of Option 1 and Option 2. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) due to the loop over m, but in the full solution, it's called O(n^3) times, leading to O(n^4) total. Space complexity is O(1) per call, but overall O(n^3) for storage. The connection to L0 is that this sub-routine is repeatedly used to fill the DP table for the full problem. Common mistakes include forgetting to add 1 to k in Option 1 (since boxes[r] itself counts), or incorrectly handling the indices in Option 2 (e.g., using m instead of m+1). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: function compute_dp(l, r, k, boxes, dp): if l > r: return 0. if dp[l][r][k] already computed, return it. Option1 = (k+1)**2 + compute_dp(l, r-1, 0, boxes, dp). Option2 = 0. for m from l to r-1: if boxes[m] == boxes[r]: Option2 = max(Option2, compute_dp(l, m, k+1, boxes, dp) + compute_dp(m+1, r-1, 0, boxes, dp)). dp[l][r][k] = max(Option1, Option2). return dp[l][r][k].

examples.trace: Compute dp[0][0][2]. boxes[0]=1. Option1: (2+1)^2=9 + dp[0][-1][0]. Since l>r for dp[0][-1][0], return 0. So Option1=9. Option2: no m in [0, -1], so 0. Thus dp[0][0][2]=9.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Partitioning Intervals at Matching Colors for Optimal Merging
ladders.desc: The technique of splitting an interval at indices with same color as the right end to combine attached boxes.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the key insight that enables solving the Remove Boxes problem efficiently. The challenge is that boxes of the same color can be far apart, and removing them together yields higher points due to the quadratic scoring (points = count^2). The intuition is to use an extra dimension k in the DP state to represent attached boxes of the same color as the right end of the interval. This allows us to defer removal and merge these attached boxes with matching boxes inside the interval. Specifically, for an interval [l, r] with k attached boxes of color boxes[r], we consider partitioning at an index m where boxes[m] matches boxes[r]. By doing so, we can solve [l, m] with k+1 attached boxes (including boxes[r] as attached) and [m+1, r-1] with 0 attached boxes, then combine the results. This partition effectively allows boxes[m] to be removed together with the attached boxes and boxes[r], maximizing points. The algorithm involves iterating over all such m and taking the maximum sum of subproblems. This logic handles non-local dependencies without needing to consider all subsets, making the DP feasible. Time complexity for this partitioning step is O(n) per state, as we check up to n indices. Space complexity is O(1) per call. The connection to L1 is that this is the Option 2 in the key sub-routine. Common mistakes include partitioning at non-matching colors, which doesn't help, or incorrectly adjusting k (e.g., not adding 1 for boxes[r]). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: For a given l, r, k, boxes: max_points = 0. For m from l to r-1: if boxes[m] == boxes[r]: points = dp(l, m, k+1) + dp(m+1, r-1, 0). max_points = max(max_points, points). This max_points is used in the DP recurrence.

examples.output: Partition points: for m=0, boxes[0]==1, compute dp(0,0,1)+dp(1,1,0).
examples.trace: boxes=[1,2,1], r=2, boxes[r]=1. Check m from 0 to 1. m=0: boxes[0]=1 matches. Compute dp(0,0,1): interval [0,0] with k=1, boxes[0]=1. Option1: (1+1)^2=4 + dp(0,-1,0)=0 ->4. Option2: none. So dp=4. dp(1,1,0): interval [1,1] with k=0, boxes[1]=2. Option1: 1^2=1 + dp(1,0,0)=0 ->1. Sum=5. m=1: boxes[1]=2 does not match, skip. So partition gives max_points=5.

examples.output: Partition points: for m=0, boxes[0]==1, compute dp(0,0,1)+dp(1,2,0).
examples.trace: boxes=[1,2,2,1], r=3, boxes[r]=1. m=0: boxes[0]=1 matches. dp(0,0,1): as above, 4. dp(1,2,0): interval [1,2] with k=0, boxes[2]=2. Compute: Option1: 1^2=1 + dp(1,1,0)=1 ->2. Option2: m=1: boxes[1]=2 matches, dp(1,1,1)+dp(2,1,0). dp(1,1,1): (1+1)^2=4 + dp(1,0,0)=0 ->4. dp(2,1,0)=0. Sum=4. So dp(1,2,0)=4. Thus partition sum=4+4=8. m=1 and m=2: no match. So max_points=8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Interval DP with Basic State dp[l][r]
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming over intervals to solve problems where subproblems depend on contiguous segments.
ladders.explanation: Interval DP is a technique where we define a DP state based on an interval [l, r] and solve subproblems for all intervals, typically in increasing length order. This building block is essential for the Remove Boxes problem because the removal of boxes affects contiguous segments. The intuition is that many problems involving sequences or arrays can be broken down into smaller intervals, and the solution for a larger interval can be computed from solutions of its sub-intervals. For example, in matrix chain multiplication or palindrome partitioning, interval DP is used. Here, we extend it with an extra dimension k. The basic algorithm for interval DP involves initializing a 2D table dp[l][r] and filling it for intervals of length 1, then 2, up to n. For each interval, we consider splitting it at some point m and combine results from dp[l][m] and dp[m+1][r]. Time complexity is often O(n^3) due to nested loops over l, r, and m. Space complexity is O(n^2). In the context of Remove Boxes, the basic interval DP would use state dp[l][r] for the maximum points from interval [l, r], but this is insufficient because it doesn't account for attached boxes of the same color outside the interval. Thus, we enhance it to dp[l][r][k]. The connection to L2 is that this provides the foundation for partitioning intervals, which is extended with the extra dimension. Common mistakes include incorrect loop orders (e.g., not processing by increasing length) or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode for a generic interval DP: n = len(arr). dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]. For length from 1 to n: For l from 0 to n-length: r = l + length - 1. If length == 1: dp[l][r] = base case. Else: dp[l][r] = 0. For m from l to r-1: dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], dp[l][m] + dp[m+1][r] + combine cost). Return dp[0][n-1].

examples.input: arr = [1,2,3], problem: maximum sum of subarrays (for illustration)
arr = [5,5], problem: remove boxes basic (without k), but incorrect for full problem
examples.trace: n=2. dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1. Length 2: l=0,r=1: boxes[0]=5, boxes[1]=5. If we remove together: 2^2=4. If separately: 1+1=2. So dp[0][1]=4. This shows the need for k to handle merging.

testCases.input: arr=[1], problem: max points from single box
arr=[1,2], problem: max points from two boxes
arr=[1,1], problem: max points from two same boxes

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Points from Removing Same-Colored Boxes
ladders.desc: Calculate points as the square of the number of same-colored boxes removed together.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to the Remove Boxes problem. Points are awarded based on the rule: if you remove a group of boxes of the same color, you get points equal to the square of the number of boxes in that group. For example, removing 3 boxes of color red gives 3^2 = 9 points. The intuition is that the quadratic scoring incentivizes removing larger groups of same-colored boxes together, rather than individually, since the points increase super-linearly. Mathematically, for a group of size x, points = x^2. This operation is used in the DP recurrence when we remove boxes[r] along with k attached boxes, yielding (k+1)^2 points. It's a simple computation but crucial for the optimization. In the algorithm, this appears in Option 1 of the DP state calculation. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). The connection to L3 is that this scoring is applied when combining intervals or making removal decisions in interval DP. Common mistakes include miscalculating the square (e.g., using 2*x instead of x^2) or forgetting to include the box itself in the count (e.g., using k instead of k+1). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def points(count): return count * count. Used as: if removing a group of size count, add points(count) to the total score.

examples.trace: Count = 1. Points = 1^2 = 1.
Count = 4. Points = 4^2 = 16.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic manipulation of arrays, including accessing elements and defining intervals.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves working with arrays, which are ordered collections of elements. In programming, an array is indexed starting from 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. An interval [l, r] in an array refers to the contiguous elements from index l to index r inclusive. For example, in array boxes = [1,3,2], the interval [0,2] includes all elements: 1,3,2. The interval [1,1] includes only the element 3. Understanding how to access elements (e.g., boxes[i] for index i) and iterate over intervals (e.g., using loops from l to r) is essential for implementing DP solutions. This concept is prerequisite because the Remove Boxes problem uses arrays to represent boxes, and the DP state relies on intervals defined by indices l and r. Beginners should practice creating arrays, accessing elements, and writing loops to traverse intervals. Time complexity for accessing an element is O(1). Space complexity for storing an array is O(n). The connection to L4 is that operations on arrays are used to compute points for groups of boxes. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using r+1 in a loop when r is inclusive) or confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: boxes = [1,3,2]. l = 0. r = 2. For i in range(l, r+1): print(boxes[i]). This prints 1,3,2.

examples.trace: Indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Loop from 0 to 2 inclusive: access boxes[0]=5, boxes[1]=10, boxes[2]=15.
Indices: 1->1, 2->1, 3->1. Loop from 1 to 3 inclusive: access boxes[1]=1, boxes[2]=1, boxes[3]=1.
